coredata.dc:title: Garlic capped silver nanoparticles for rapid detection of cholesterol
coredata.dc:creator: El-Naka, Marwa Ahmed
El-Dissouky, A.
Ali, G.Y.
Ebrahim, Shaker
Shokry, Azza
coredata.dc:description: A fluorescent biosensor based on garlic (Allium sativum L.) capped Ag nanoparticles (G-Ag NPs) has been synthesized for cholesterol detection. Pristine Ag NPs and G-Ag NPs were synthesized through the chemical reduction process. The effect of different capping agents such as 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTS), glutathione, 8-hydroxyquinoline, garlic/APTS, garlic/glutathione, and garlic/8-hydroxyquinoline on Ag NPs was evaluated. These NPs were characterized using Fourier transform infrared (FTIR), energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), X-Ray diffraction (XRD), UV–visible spectra, and Zeta potential. The HRTEM micrographs illustrated that Ag NPs with particles size ranging from 2.98 to 14.34 nm were aggregated. G-Ag NPs images showed uniformly distributed spherical particles with particles size from 4.52 to 12.8 nm. The reduction in the plasmonic bands of Ag NPs and G-Ag NPs occurred by 96.4% and 11.7%, respectively after 12 months. The developed sensor for cholesterol based on the fluorescence enhancement had a linear response in a concentration range of 0.4–5.17 mM with a sensitivity of 4.36 Mm−1 and a limit of detection of 0.186 mM. The high selectivity toward cholesterol in presence of different interferes such as glucose, cysteine, glycine, urea, sucrose, nickel, and copper, and their mixture was evaluated. The applicability of this developed sensor for real serum samples was detected with a recovery percentage from 99.1 to 101.3%. Repeatability and reproducibility experiments displayed relative standard deviations (RSD) of 0.88% and 0.62%, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Garlic extract
Silver nanoparticles
Fluorescence
Sensor
Cholesterol

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of Ag NPs, G-Ag NPs, and other different capping agents
Detection of cholesterol in real blood samples in the medical laboratory
Detection of cholesterol in real blood serum samples using G-Ag NPs
Structural properties of Ag NPs and G-Ag NPs
Crystallinity studies of Ag NPs and G-Ag NPs
Morphological properties of Ag NPs and G-Ag NPs
Selectivity and interferences of G-Ag NPs
Detection of cholesterol in real blood serum samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FTIR spectra of Ag NPs and G-Ag NPs.
EDX spectra of Ag NPs (a) and G-Ag NPs (b).
XRD patterns of Ag NPs (a) and G-Ag NPs (b).

HRTEM images of Ag NPs (a), G-Ag NPs (b), high magnified Ag NPs. Inset is d spacing of Ag NPs (C), high magnified G-Ag NPs. Inset is d spacing of G-Ag NPs (d), electron diffraction patterns of Ag NPs (e), and G-Ag NPs (f).
UV–Vis spectra of Ag NPs (a), G-Ag NPs (b), Tauc plots of Ag NPs (c), and G-Ag NPs (d).
Zeta potential charts of Ag NPs (a) and G-Ag NPs(b).
The measured and recovered cholesterol in human serum samples using G-Ag NPs biosensor.

head.ce:title: Garlic capped silver nanoparticles for rapid detection of cholesterol

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry Department, Faculty of Science, Alexandria University, P.O. Box 426, Ibrahimia, 21321, Alexandria, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chemistry Department, Faculty of Science, Alexandria University, P.O. Box 426, Ibrahimia, 21321, Alexandria, Egypt

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science, Institute of Graduate Studies and Research, Alexandria University, P.O. Box 832, Alexandria, Egypt

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Graduate Studies and Research

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Materials Science, Institute of Graduate Studies and Research, Alexandria University, P.O. Box 832, Alexandria, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A fluorescent biosensor based on garlic (Allium sativum L.) capped Ag nanoparticles (G-Ag NPs) has been synthesized for cholesterol detection. Pristine Ag NPs and G-Ag NPs were synthesized through the chemical reduction process. The effect of different capping agents such as 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTS), glutathione, 8-hydroxyquinoline, garlic/APTS, garlic/glutathione, and garlic/8-hydroxyquinoline on Ag NPs was evaluated. These NPs were characterized using Fourier transform infrared (FTIR), energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), X-Ray diffraction (XRD), UV–visible spectra, and Zeta potential. The HRTEM micrographs illustrated that Ag NPs with particles size ranging from 2.98 to 14.34 nm were aggregated. G-Ag NPs images showed uniformly distributed spherical particles with particles size from 4.52 to 12.8 nm. The reduction in the plasmonic bands of Ag NPs and G-Ag NPs occurred by 96.4% and 11.7%, respectively after 12 months. The developed sensor for cholesterol based on the fluorescence enhancement had a linear response in a concentration range of 0.4–5.17 mM with a sensitivity of 4.36 Mm−1 and a limit of detection of 0.186 mM. The high selectivity toward cholesterol in presence of different interferes such as glucose, cysteine, glycine, urea, sucrose, nickel, and copper, and their mixture was evaluated. The applicability of this developed sensor for real serum samples was detected with a recovery percentage from 99.1 to 101.3%. Repeatability and reproducibility experiments displayed relative standard deviations (RSD) of 0.88% and 0.62%, respectively.

The suggested mechanisms of interaction between cholesterol and G-Ag NPs.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Cholesterol is a critical lipid for mammalian cell membranes and plays a significant role in ensuring proper membrane integrity and fluidity [1]. Cholesterol has anticancer properties and exists in the immune system and synapses in the brain. Healthy persons have a blood cholesterol level of approximately 5.2 mM L−1 (200 mg dL−1). High cholesterol levels result in coronary heart diseases, atherosclerosis, hypertension, stroke, and lipid metabolism disorders [1,2]. On the other hand, cholesterol concentrations below 167 mg dL−1 cause hypocholesterolemia. Therefore, there are different strategies proposed for the detection of cholesterol for easy diagnosis and/or treatment of the above-mentioned diseases [3].

Various techniques have been applied for cholesterol detection including high-performance thin layer chromatography [4], colorimetric [1,2], photonic crystal [5], and fluorescence method [4]. The fluorescence technique is characterized by high sensitivity, quick response time, and good selectivity [7].

Ag NPs have attracted increasing interest because of their optical, and electrical properties, which can incorporate into raw materials for antimicrobial, electronics, water treatment, and sensors applications [8–12]. Surface modification of Ag NPs can be performed through different methods, stabilizers, and reducing agents. The chemical reduction method for the preparation of Ag NPs is a felicitous and simple technique to tune the size and properties of Ag NPs [13].

Garlic (Allium sativum L.) is a plant applied in medicine and cooking. The aqueous extracts of garlic contain various phytoconstituents such as phenolic compounds, glycosides, carbohydrates, flavonoids, tannins, saponins, proteins, and amino acids [14]. However, garlic extract contains allicin or diallyl thiosulphate in a high concentration. Allicin as an active compound involves carbon double bonds where chemical bonds with other compounds can be formed. The sulfur atom has a good affinity to silver. Furthermore, since allicin is a negatively charged compound, it can be used as a capping agent to create nanosized particles [14,15]. Therefore, garlic is used in this research as a powerful capping agent for Ag NPs.

This work aims to prepare pristine Ag NPs and G-Ag NPs. The structural, optical, surface properties, and morphological characteristics are studied using infrared, XPS, EDX, XRD, UV–visible spectra, photoluminescence spectra, zeta potential, and HRTEM techniques. The fluorescence property of G-Ag NPs is used to determine the linearity, dynamic range, sensitivity, and the limit of detection for cholesterol in the range from 0.4 mM to 5.17 mM. The pH effect on the fluorescence efficiency and intensity of G-Ag NPs is investigated in the pH range from 2 to 10. The selectivity and interference of G-Ag NPs in the presence of some potential interfering substances are examined and evaluated. The proposed mechanisms for the interaction between G-Ag NPs and cholesterol are also discussed.

Silver nitrate (99.8%) was purchased from PRS Panreac, Spain. Sodium borohydride (99.0%) and 8-hydroxyquinoline (99%) were received from Merck, Germany. Garlic was purchased from a local market. Cholesterol standard was received from Egy-chem Co., Egypt. Phosphate buffer pH 7 (PBS) was received from Chem-Lab Co., Belgium. Triton X-100 was obtained from Luba-Chem, India. Ethanol (99.9%), Sucrose (99%), and urea (99%) were supplied by El-Nasr Co., Egypt. Copper chloride (99%), nickel chloride (99%) and 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTS) (99%) were obtained by Acros Organics, Belgium. Isopropanol (99.5%) was supplied from Al-Alamia Co., Egypt. Human serum samples are obtained from a local medical laboratory. d-Glucose and glycine (99%) were obtained from BDH chemicals Ltd, England. L-cysteine hydrochloride monohydrate (98.5–101%) and glutathione reduced (98%) were obtained from Fisher Scientific, United States.

Ten milligrams of garlic cloves were washed with deionized water and ground with mortar. Then, 100 mL of deionized water was added. The resultant solution was filtered to remove small garlic pieces and the white clear solution of garlic extract was collected. Then, this filtrate was concentrated by heating at 40 °C until the volume was 92 mL of garlic extract. The garlic extract was stored at 4 °C for further use. The garlic extract concentration was determined to be 1.6 mM by measuring the weight of the residual solid (0.08 g) after evaporating 100 mL of the solution in an oven at 40 °C [16,17].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Ag NPs, G-Ag NPs, and other different capping agents
ce:section.ce:para: Ag NPs and G-Ag NPs were synthesized through the silver nitrate chemical reduction using sodium borohydride. Sodium borohydride (1.2 mL) with 10 mM was added to a conical flask containing deionized water (36.8 mL) and placed in an ice bath with a vigorously stirring. Then, 10 mM silver nitrate solution (0.4 mL) was added drop wisely (1 drop/second). The resultant solution color progressively turned to yellow which indicated the formation of Ag NPs. For the preparation of G-Ag NPs, the previous steps were followed by the dropwise addition of 1.6 mM garlic extract (1 mL) as a capping agent with a continuous stirring of the mixture for another 10 min. Ag NPs and G- Ag NPs powders were collected using centrifugation process (centrifuge focus serial No: 1107, Spain) at 7000 rpm for 15 min. Then, the collected powders were washed several times using deionized water and ethanol, respectively, then dried at room temperature [18].

To evaluate the efficiency of garlic as a capping agent, the same procedure was repeated to produce Ag NPs capped with 1.6 mM of APTS, glutathione reduced (glutathione), 8-hydroxyquinoline, garlic/APTS, garlic/glutathione and garlic/8-hydroxyquinoline [18].

UV–vis absorption spectra have been detected using a UV–visible spectrophotometer (Evolution 300, Thermo Scientific, USA). The structural and functional groups investigation was carried out using FTIR (Spectrum BX 11- LX 18–5255 PerkinElmer). Particle size, morphology, and selected area electron diffraction (SAED) were examined using HRTEM (JEOL, JEM-2100 LaB6). The crystallinity of the synthesized nanoparticles was detected by XRD (Bruker- AXS D8 Discover) at room temperature. The surface charge was detected using Zeta Potential (Zetasizer Nano ZS Malvern). Fluorescence measurements were carried out on a spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer LS-55). The elemental analysis was done using EDX spectroscopy on an SEM (JSM-IT 200). The electronic properties and chemical composition of Ag NPs and G-Ag NPs were evaluated using XPS (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA).

A stock solution of 5 mM of cholesterol was prepared by dissolving the standard cholesterol (5.17 mM) in a mixture of aqueous solution of 0.1 M PBS, Triton X-100, and isopropanol (8:1:1 v/v) [6]. The above cholesterol solution (0.1 mL) with various concentrations (0.4–5.17 mM) was added to 2.9 mL DI and 0.4 mL G-Ag NPs. These solutions were mixed by a vortex mixer for 1 min and were used in the cholesterol detection experiments where pH of these solutions was 7.6.

Then, the fluorescence spectra were collected with the excitation wavelength (λex) at 270 nm. The collected emission data were in the range of 300–600 nm. For the quantification of cholesterol, the maximum fluorescence intensity was detected and recorded at 310 nm.

The experimental detection conditions were optimized by studying the effect of pH and the response time between G-Ag NPs and cholesterol. Also, the selectivity of G-Ag NPs towards cholesterol was studied by measuring the luminescence intensity of G-Ag NPs in the presence of a variety of other relevant substances that are present in blood serum such as glucose, cysteine, glycine, urea, sucrose, nickel, and copper (5 mM). The analytical parameters were optimized and carried out through the following equation (2) [7,19]:
(1)
(
F −
F ∘
)
/
F ∘
=
aC
c h o l
+ b
where F and F° refer to the PL intensity of G-Ag NPs in the presence and absence of cholesterol, respectively, Cchol represents the concentration of cholesterol, and a and b are slope and intercept of the calibration curve, respectively. The fluorescence enhancement effect (FE) was calculated as shown in equation (2) [19].
(2)
FE =
( F − F ° )
/ F °

Also, the limit of detection (LOD) of cholesterol was determined using equation (3) [20].
(3)
LOD =
3 SD
S
where S and SD are slope and standard deviation, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of cholesterol in real blood samples in the medical laboratory
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the concentration of cholesterol in blood samples, a standard method using an automated spectrophotometer (CS-T240 AUTO-Chemistry Analyzer) was used. Before the cholesterol detection, calibration curve was carried out. In the calibration stage, a certain reagent (cholesterol reacts with this reagent to produce an adduct that absorbs a unique wavelength of 546 nm) was added to each cuvette and then incubated at 37 °C. The calibration standards which are solutions with known concentrations of cholesterol are added to the reagent, mixed, and incubated. The absorbance resulting from the reaction of the reagent and standard was measured with the automated spectrophotometer. Finally, a calibration curve is sketched from the standard concentrations and their absorption data. Five blood samples were provided from different patients and then was centrifuged for 30 min to separate serum from the whole blood. The same steps in the calibration stage were repeated but the serum samples were used instead of the calibration standards. Then, the light absorption was measured for each serum sample and the concentration was calculated from the calibration curve in the range from 2.48 to 3.95 mM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of cholesterol in real blood serum samples using G-Ag NPs
ce:section.ce:para:
The efficiency of G-Ag NPs for the determination of cholesterol levels was tested by using the five human blood serum samples taken from the local medical laboratory. For the PL measurements of the blood serum samples, 0.1 mL aliquot of serum was added to 0.4 mL of G-Ag NPs and 2.9 mL deionized water, mixed well with a vortex mixer for 1 min, and incubated for 5 min. The recovery percentage was also calculated using equation (4) [21].
(4)
Recovery
( % )
=
clinical conc . provided
found conc .
× 100

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural properties of Ag NPs and G-Ag NPs
ce:section.ce:para: The functional groups of Ag NPs and G-Ag NPs are detected by FTIR and illustrated in Fig. 1 . G-Ag NPs spectrum displays the same peaks of Ag NPs with small shifts in position and changes in the band intensities owing to the strong interaction between G-Ag NPs and Ag NPs. This confirms that garlic acts as a capping agent and prevents G-Ag NPs agglomeration as well as providing the desired stability [22]. The peak at 3461 cm−1 is shifted to 3390 cm−1 which refers to NH group, the OH stretching of water, phenolic compounds, and flavonoids of garlic extract [14,22,23]. The peak of OH bending vibration of water located at 1633 cm−1 is switched to 1637 cm−1 in G-Ag NPs [24]. Also, this peak is related to the CC group of allicin as a major component of garlic extract [24]. The peak of the NO2 group at 1386 cm−1 is switched to 1405 cm−1. The band correlated to Ag NPs stretch at 525 cm−1 is switched to 530 cm−1 [25]. The two bands at 2936 and 1130 cm−1 of G-Ag NPs are related to the C–H stretching vibrations of garlic [26]. Also, the peak at 1289 cm−1 is ascribed to the stretching vibration of C–S of garlic (allicin) [16]. The SO stretching vibration peak of allicin is located at 1027 cm−1 [25].

XPS technique is adopted to indicate the chemical and electronic structure at the molecule–metal interface and the stability of the capped nanoparticles [27]. Fig. S1 (S, supplementary material) shows the XPS used to differentiate functional group alteration of Ag NPs and G-Ag NPs. The XPS survey of Ag NPs and G-Ag NPs (Fig. S1a) proves the presence of Ag and O. It is also noted that the XPS intensity of Ag NPS is higher than that of G-Ag NPs as a result of the interaction between garlic and Ag NPs [22]. For Ag NPs, doublet intense (Ag 3d5/2 and Ag 3d3/2) is detected at 386.51 and 374.46 eV binding energies, respectively (Fig. S1b) [28]. Also, the O1s spectra in Fig. S1c show one peak at 531.88 eV indicating the presence of adsorbed water on the Ag NPs surface [29]. In case of G-Ag NPs, the resultant elemental scans illustrate the presence of Ag, C, O, and S elements as depicted in Figs. S1d–g. Fig. S1d displays the doublet intense of Ag 3d5/2 and Ag 3d3/2 in G-Ag NPs at 368.46 and 374.18 eV, respectively and this confirms the presence of the metallic Ag (zero oxidation state) [28]. The XPS results emphasize that NaBH4 has succeeded to reduce silver ions into metallic silver [28,30]. Moreover, garlic has been characterized by three elements (C 1s, O 1s, S 2p), and from Fig. S1e, the C 1s spectra are expressed by 5 peaks due to the different chemical environments around carbon. The main peak at 284.68 eV (73.5%) refers to (C–C), (C–H) and (CC) of allicin in garlic extract [30,31]. The second peak at 286.08 eV assigns to C–S of allicin [32]. The third peak at 286.88 eV corresponds to the C–O of garlic extract [33]. The fourth peak at 287.68 is attributed to CO [31]. Finally, a very weak peak at 288.98 eV refers to O–CO belonging to carboxylic groups of garlic extract [34]. Fig. S1f shows two peaks of O 1s, the first one at 531.2 eV is equivalent to hydroxyl groups and the second peak at 532.64 eV is assigned to adsorbed water and OS [29,35,36]. Fig. S1g displays a doublet for S 2p related to S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 spectra appearing at binding energies of 161.28 and 163.38 eV corresponding to the bond of C–S and S–S in allicin [36–38].

The elemental analysis of Ag NPs and G-Ag NPs was performed using EDX and illustrated in Fig. 2a and b, respectively. Fig. 2a depicts a strong signal at 3 keV which confirms the complete Ag+1 ions reduction by NaBH4 to Ag NPs [39]. Likewise, the EDX spectra of G-Ag NPs (Fig. 2b) illustrates a strong sharp peak at 3 keV because of Ag+1 ions reduction by NaBH4 to Ag NPs. The moderate peaks at 0.25 keV and 0.51 keV are assigned to C-atom (13.48 wt%) and O-atom (21.0 wt%) of garlic. The small peak at 2.3 keV refers to S-atom (1.14 wt%) in garlic. The appearance of the peaks of C, O, and S atoms emphasize the presence of the garlic extract on the Ag NPs surface [15].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Crystallinity studies of Ag NPs and G-Ag NPs
ce:section.ce:para: XRD is used to detect the crystalline nature and the structural properties of the synthesized Ag NPs and G-Ag NPs. For Ag NPs, the XRD pattern (Fig. 3 a) displays three peaks at 2θ values of 37.45°, 43.56°, and 63.83°, which are indexed to planes (111), (200), and (220), respectively [40–45]. Also, for G-Ag NPs, the XRD pattern (Fig. 3b) exhibits almost the same characteristic peaks at the same position. The d-spacings obtained for Ag NPs and G-Ag NPs are 2.399 Å (0.24 nm) and 2.391 Å (0.24 nm), respectively, which are nearly close to the results obtained from HRTEM images (Insets of Fig. 4c and d) [45].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological properties of Ag NPs and G-Ag NPs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of the prepared Ag NPs and G-Ag NPs are illustrated by HRTEM at different magnifications as demonstrated in Fig. 4. The image of Ag NPs (Fig. 4a) reveals that Ag NPs are agglomerated and nearly spherical with particles diameter ranging from 2.98 nm to 14.34 nm. The HRTEM image of G-Ag NPs depicted in Fig. 4b confirms the presence of uniformly distributed spherical particles thanks to the capping garlic extract [41,42]. The diameter size of these nanoparticles is estimated to be in the range of 4.52–12.8 nm. It is observed that the synthesized Ag NPs and G- Ag NPs have small particles size and can be considered in the range of quantum dots. In addition, HRTEM images and insets of Fig. 4 c and d show the same lattice fringe indicating that Ag NPs and G- Ag NPs are highly crystalline structures with a d-spacing of 0.22 nm. The electron diffraction patterns (Fig. 4 e and f) confirm that both Ag NPs and G-Ag NPs are polycrystalline materials [46].

The absorption properties of Ag NPs and G-Ag NPs are investigated and illustrated in Fig. 5 (a and b). Ag NPs display a strong absorption band at 390 nm (Fig. 5a) related to the electrons surface plasmon resonance in the conduction band [47,48]. The plasmon band is narrow and symmetrical in shape which confirms that Ag NPs have restricted size distribution [46]. After the addition of garlic extract to the Ag NPs, there is a slight red shift in the absorption band of G-Ag NPs from 390 to 400 nm (Fig. 5b), which confirms the strong interaction between Ag NPs and garlic extract [22,23]. Compared to pristine Ag NPs, the shape of the plasmon band of G-Ag NPs is wider as a result of the interaction between the garlic extract and Ag NPs as shown in Fig. 5 b [46–48].

The stability of Ag NPs and G-Ag NPs was detected by measuring the intensity of their absorption peaks over 12 months. It is observed that there is a drop in the peak intensity of Ag NPs by 57% after 1 week and by 96.4% after 12 months. (Fig. 5a). In contrast, the peak intensity of G-Ag NPs presents high stability with a reduction of about 11.7% after 12 months (Fig. 5b).

The values of band gap energy (Eg) for Ag NPs and G-Ag NPs are calculated from Tauc's relationship, in which α is the optical absorption coefficient, hѵ refers to the photon energy and A is constant as shown in equation (5) [49,50].
(5)
α h ν
= A
( h ν
- E
g
)
n

The tauc plots in Fig. 5c and d illustrate the estimated band gap for both Ag NPs and G-Ag NPs as 2.95 and 2.83 eV, respectively. These values indicate that Ag NPs and G-Ag NPs have dielectric behavior which induces polarization when intense radiation is incident on NPs and confirms the suitability for photonic and optical applications [49,50].

Fig. 6 (a and b) presents the zeta potential of Ag NPs and G-Ag NPs. The potential value of Ag NPs is −15.2 mV. According to G-Ag NPs, the chart illustrates that the value of zeta potential increased to - 22 mV. The increase in the negative charge denotes more stable nanoparticles as a result of the interaction between the garlic extract and Ag NPs [16].

To study the emission properties of G-Ag NPs, the PL spectra of G-Ag NPs aqueous solution is measured at different excitation wavelengths (λex) from 270 to 350 nm at room temperature (Fig. S2a). G-Ag NPs exhibit an excitation-independent emission at 360 nm. At an excitation wavelength of 270 nm, the maximum fluorescence intensity is obtained and declined by increasing the excitation wavelength. This behavior is related to the enhancement of surface defects reduction through nonradiative recombination of holes and electrons [6].

The stability of Ag NPs is pH-dependent, so even adding a small volume of acids (such as HCl) can prevent the growth of Ag NPs [51]. The PL intensity of G-Ag NPs is detected in the pH range from 2 to 10 as shown in Fig. S2b. To study the effect of pH on PL of G-Ag NPs, 0.4 mL of G-Ag NPs is added to 3 mL of phosphate buffer solutions with pH 7.1.0 M HCl and 0.1 M NaOH were used to adjust the pH. It is revealed that the PL intensity of G-Ag NPs is enhanced by rising the solution pH, suggesting that the growth and stability of Ag NPs take place through an electrostatic stabilization mechanism [51,52]. The pH-sensitive groups such as different types of ἀ-amino acids (the main structure of proteins) and carboxylic groups are presented in aqueous garlic extract [51]. Moreover, by increasing the pH, the percentage of free HS-group of allicin and cysteine active species in the garlic extract increase, which as a result increase the growth rates and the PL intensity [51]. However, it is found that the PL peak of the blank (G-Ag NPs in distilled water with pH of 7.6) is the sharpest and the highest intensity. In addition, the PL peaks are red shifted in the other solutions. These defects can be attributed to the use of buffer solution [51].

To evaluate the efficiency of garlic as a capping agent, the same procedure is repeated to produce Ag NPs capped with 1.6 mM of APTS, glutathione, garlic/APTS, garlic/glutathione, and garlic/8-hydroxyquinoline, then their PL spectra are compared as shown in Fig. S3. Glutathione and APTS capped Ag NPs exhibited main broad peaks at 425 nm and shoulder peaks at 487 nm. Garlic/glutathione, garlic/APTS and garlic/8-hydroxyquinoline capped Ag NPs also showed two peaks at 425 nm and 487 nm. Besides, the characteristic peak of G-Ag NPs changed to be broader and red-shifted from 360 nm to 380 nm. Using garlic as a capping agent for Ag NPs produces the highest PL intensity and sharpest peak among other examined capping agents [46].

For further investigation of the fluorescence stability of the synthesized G-Ag NPs over a long time (20 weeks), the PL intensity of the G-Ag NPs solution is tested after being stored at room temp (∼30 °C). As demonstrated in Fig. S4, the PL intensity of G-Ag NPs is slightly decayed by 6.7%, 11.6%, and 27.4% after four, eight, and twenty weeks, respectively. The high stability of G-Ag NPs is due to their high zeta potential value [46].

The excited photoluminance of G-Ag NPs is the main factor for its application as a sensor for cholesterol. Fig. S5 (a and b) illustrates the effect of incubation time of 0.4 mM cholesterol with G-Ag NPs on the PL intensity in which the enhancement efficiency attains the equilibrium state within nearly 2 min, and the PL intensity remains fixed after this time [53]. These results revealed that G-Ag NPs are reactive toward cholesterol which means that G-Ag NPs achieve rapid detection and are also very stable. As depicted in Fig. S6a, in the absence of cholesterol, the PL intensity of G-Ag NPs is low, while by increasing the cholesterol concentration from 0.4 to 5.17 mM, the PL intensity of G-Ag NPs is gradually enhanced. A strong blue shift of about 46.5 nm is observed indicating that a complex between cholesterol and G-Ag NPs is formed [7]. Fig. S6b depicts a linear relationship between fluorescence enhancement effect (FE) and cholesterol concentration with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9986, limit of detection (LOD) of 0.186 mM, and sensitivity of 4.36 Mm−1.

The enhancement effect of G-Ag NPs by cholesterol is suggested by two proposed mechanisms as illustrated in Fig. S7 depending on the reactivity of the allicin of garlic which acts as a capping agent for Ag NPs. In the first mechanism, ionic bonds between sulfur cation of allicin and oxygen anion of cholesterol can be formed. The other mechanism of PL enhancement is the hydrogen bonding formation between the oxygen atom of allicin and the hydrogen of the hydroxyl group of cholesterol [15,16].

The repeatability of the prepared G-Ag NPs is measured after six consecutive PL measurements of the same G-Ag NPs with 4.6 mM cholesterol as shown in Fig. S8a. The relative standard deviation (RSD) is detected to be 0.88%. Also, the reproducibility of the synthesized biosensor is detected after measuring the PL intensity of six different G-Ag NPs samples added to 1.2 mM cholesterol solutions prepared independently (Fig. S8b) and the RSD is detected to be 0.62%. These results emphasize that the G-Ag NPs biosensor is applicable for repeatable experiments and highly reproducible [55].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and interferences of G-Ag NPs
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of the fabricated biosensor is detected in the presence of potentially interfering substances, such as glucose, urea, glycine, CuCl2, NiCl2, sucrose, L-cysteine hydrochloride, and their mixture which already co-exist with cholesterol in the blood serum. Figs. S9a and b illustrate the PL response of G-Ag NPs after treatment with (5 mM) of different interferents. It is found that CuCl2, sucrose, NiCl2, glycine, cysteine, and glucose have a small quenching effect on the PL response of G-Ag NPs. On the other hand, urea has a moderate enhancement effect on the PL response. Even though adding a large concentration of interferents (nearly double the amount of cholesterol), there is a slight change in the PL response of G-Ag NPs, while adding a small concentration of cholesterol (2.7 mM) has a strong enhancement effect on the PL response. From the previous results, G-Ag NPs as a biosensor were found to be very selective to cholesterol and can be applied in real blood samples [54].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of cholesterol in real blood serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: We tested the effectivity of G-Ag NPs as a biosensor of cholesterol in real human blood samples. Five human serum samples and their clinical cholesterol level data are taken from a local medical laboratory. Then, the PL is measured for each serum sample (Fig. S10) from which the concentration of cholesterol can be detected using the calibration curve in Fig. S6b. The PL intensity is measured 4 times for each serum sample. Table (1) shows the detected cholesterol concentration, the clinical data given by the medical laboratory, the recoveries which are calculated and found in the range of 99.1–101.3%, and RSD which is found in the range of 0.82–1.5% [21]. These results confirmed that G-Ag NPS can be used as an efficient and accurate detector of cholesterol levels in real blood samples.

In this paper, the successful preparation of both Ag NPs and G-Ag NPs was achieved and confirmed by using different characterization techniques. The effect of different capping agents such as APTS, glutathione, 8-hydroxyquinoline, garlic/APTS, garlic/glutathione, and garlic/8-hydroxyquinoline was evaluated. G-Ag NPs were used as a sensitive fluorescence probe for the cholesterol detection in the range of 0.4–5.17 mM providing a low LOD of 0.186 mM and sensitivity of 4.36 Mm−1. Furthermore, the G-Ag NPs biosensor offered a fast response time which is nearly 2 min, and good selectivity in presence of other interferents such as glucose, cysteine, glycine, urea, sucrose, nickel, and copper. Also, the G-Ag NPs biosensor displayed high stability over more than one year and good reproducibility and repeatability with RSD of 0.62% and 0.88% respectively. The calculated recovery percentages of cholesterol were found in the range of 99.1–101.3% which confirmed that G-Ag NPS can be used in cholesterol sensing.

Marwa A. El-Naka: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Writing - original draft. A. El-Dissouky: Conceptualization - Supervision - Review & Editing. G. Y. Ali: Supervision - Review & Editing. Shaker Ebrahim: Conceptualization, Supervision, Data curation, Formal analysis, Review & Editing. Azza Shokry: Conceptualization, Supervision, Data curation, Formal analysis, Review & Editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para:

Rep. 11 (2021) 13969, https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-021-93399-y.
